section.title: Practical Warnings
section.sectionRationale: A cluster of four short, practical warnings breaks from the sustained argument style of the surrounding discourses: reckless financial pledges, verses 1 to 5, laziness taught by the ant, verses 6 to 11, the troublemaker who sows discord, verses 12 to 15, and a numbered list of what the LORD hates, verses 16 to 19, closing the section with a clear moral summary.
section.thesis: Wisdom is intensely practical, it warns against foolish financial entanglements, exposes the sluggard's self deception, names the scheming troublemaker for what he is, and finally lists, in the LORD's own reckoning, the very things His heart detests.
section.frameNote: Four short movements: escaping a reckless pledge of security, verses 1 to 5; a lesson in diligence from the ant, verses 6 to 11; the portrait of a worthless, discord sowing man, verses 12 to 15; and the seven things the LORD hates, verses 16 to 19.

verses.text: My son, if you have put up security for your neighbor, if you have struck hands in pledge with a stranger,
if you have been trapped by the words of your lips, ensnared by the words of your mouth,
then do this, my son, to free yourself, for you have fallen into your neighbor's hands: Go, humble yourself, and press your plea with your neighbor.
Allow no sleep to your eyes or slumber to your eyelids.
Free yourself, like a gazelle from the hand of the hunter, like a bird from the snare of the fowler.
Walk in the manner of the ant, O {{atsel|slacker}}; observe its ways and become wise.
Without a commander, without an overseer or ruler,
it prepares its provisions in summer; it gathers its food at harvest.
How long will you lie there, O slacker? When will you get up from your sleep?
A little sleep, a little slumber, a little folding of the hands to rest,
and poverty will come upon you like a robber, and need like a bandit.
A {{beliyaal|worthless person}}, a wicked man, walks with a perverse mouth,
winking his eyes, speaking with his feet, and pointing with his fingers.
With deceit in his heart he devises evil; he continually sows discord.
Therefore calamity will come upon him suddenly; in an instant he will be shattered beyond recovery.
There are six things that the LORD hates, seven that are {{toebah|detestable}} to Him:
haughty eyes, a lying tongue, hands that shed innocent blood,
a heart that devises wicked schemes, feet that run swiftly to evil,
a false witness who gives false testimony, and one who stirs up discord among brothers.

atsel.gloss: The sluggard or slacker, a recurring character throughout Proverbs. Here he is sent to school by an insect, the ant, whose diligence without any external commander exposes the sluggard's self chosen, self deceiving idleness for what it is.

beliyaal.gloss: A worthless person, literally one without value or profit, a term that became a name for wickedness itself, Belial, later used of Satan, 2 Corinthians 6:15. Here he schemes with body language, winking, pointing, before his sudden, irrecoverable downfall.

toebah.gloss: Detestable or abominable, a strong term for what is morally repugnant to God, used elsewhere of idolatry and covenant breaking. The list in verses 17 to 19 shows that God's hatred is not reserved for religious offenses alone but extends to pride, dishonesty, violence, and division among His people.

basic.context: This short section gathers four separate, very practical warnings. First, if you have rashly guaranteed someone else's debt, get out of it urgently and humbly. Second, if you are lazy, learn diligence from an ant, who needs no boss to keep working. Third, beware the schemer who plots evil with sly body language and sows discord, whose sudden downfall cannot be undone. Finally, the section climaxes with a list, in God's own words, of seven things He detests, pride, lying, violence, scheming, eagerness for evil, false testimony, and stirring up discord.
basic.hermeneutics: Each warning stands on its own as concrete, situational wisdom rather than abstract theology, yet together they build toward the numbered list in verses 16 to 19, which functions as a summarizing moral compass for the whole cluster. The 'six, even seven' numbering is a common Hebrew poetic device for completeness, not a claim that only exactly seven things offend God.
basic.originalLanguages: The atsel, sluggard, is shamed by the ant's self motivated diligence, verse 6, a vivid picture used because insects were assumed to lack the reasoning capacity humans have no excuse to squander. The beliyyaal, worthless person, of verse 12 carries a weight beyond mere laziness or foolishness, it became a name for embodied wickedness, later even a title for Satan. And the list closes on things toebah, detestable, to the LORD, a strong covenantal word usually reserved for idolatry, now applied to pride, violence, and discord among God's people.
basic.typology: The false witness and the sower of discord, verse 19, anticipate the very charges brought against Christ at His trial, false witnesses testifying against Him, Matthew 26:59 to 60, so that the very sins this passage names God as hating were, in a supreme irony, used to condemn the innocent Son of God.
basic.christ: This list of things the LORD hates, haughty eyes, a lying tongue, hands that shed innocent blood, false testimony, discord among brothers, describes exactly the conspiracy that convicted Jesus, false witnesses were sought against Him, Matthew 26:59 to 60, and His innocent blood was shed by proud and scheming men. Christ bore the very sins this passage condemns, absorbing their worst expression at the cross, so that He might forgive both those who commit them and those who suffer under them.
basic.god: God's hatred here is not arbitrary, it targets pride, deceit, violence, scheming, eagerness for evil, false testimony, and division, each one an assault on the truth, on human life, or on community. This same God is patient with the sluggard and the reckless debtor, offering practical ways back rather than instant condemnation, showing that His hatred of sin coexists with real patience toward the sinner who still has time to change course.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 26:59 to 60 shows false witnesses sought against Jesus, echoing verse 19. Romans 12:18 calls believers to live at peace with everyone, opposing the discord of verse 19. James 4:6 opposes the proud, echoing the haughty eyes of verse 17. Proverbs 26:20 further develops the danger of the one who spreads strife.
basic.oneStory: This cluster of practical warnings, on debt, laziness, scheming, and God's own list of what He hates, shows that biblical wisdom reaches into the most ordinary corners of life. That same list of hated sins, false witness and shed innocent blood chief among them, was turned against the innocent Christ at His trial, so that in bearing them He might free His people from both committing and suffering under them.

context.literary: A cluster of four distinct short units rather than one sustained argument, linked by their shared concern for practical, everyday wisdom, financial entanglement, work ethic, social scheming, and a closing numbered list, a literary form, x, even x plus one, common in Hebrew wisdom poetry to signal a complete, representative set.
context.historical: Standing surety for a neighbor's debt, verses 1 to 5, was a real and serious risk in the ancient world, where a guarantor could be legally bound to a stranger's obligations with no protections like modern bankruptcy law, making this warning a matter of urgent practical survival, not abstract caution.
context.canonical: The seven things the LORD hates, verses 16 to 19, echoes covenant language used elsewhere of Israel's corporate sins, and its emphasis on false testimony and shed innocent blood connects forward to the ninth commandment and to the prophetic condemnation of judicial corruption throughout Israel's history.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To press wisdom into the most concrete corners of daily life, financial entanglement, work habits, and social conduct, and to summarize the moral heart of the matter in a list of what God Himself hates, so the reader sees these as not merely practical tips but matters of genuine importance to the LORD.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The commands to free oneself from a reckless pledge, to learn diligence, and to avoid scheming discord are directly prescriptive. The list of seven things the LORD hates, verses 16 to 19, is best read as a representative, summarizing set using a Hebrew numerical device, not an exhaustive catalogue implying God hates nothing else; the whole book, and the whole Bible, names many more sins God detests.

debates.question: Does the warning against pledging security, verses 1 to 5, forbid all financial guarantees or cosigning?

views.label: A blanket warning against ever guaranteeing another's debt
views.case: The urgency of the language, free yourself like a gazelle from a hunter, suggests the practice itself is inherently dangerous and to be avoided.

views.label: A warning against reckless, ill considered pledges specifically
views.case: The passage targets rash, trapped commitments, having struck hands with a stranger, verse 1, rather than every conceivable financial guarantee; wisdom elsewhere assumes some financial cooperation between neighbors is normal, so the concern is imprudent entanglement, not cooperative generosity as such.

debates.bearing: Read alongside the rest of Proverbs, which commends generosity and neighborly help, the wiser reading treats this as a warning against reckless, poorly considered financial entanglement, especially with strangers, rather than a blanket prohibition on ever helping to guarantee a trustworthy neighbor's need.

study.originalLanguages: The atsel, sluggard, is publicly shamed by an insect, the ant, whose diligence needs no commander, verses 6 to 7, exposing laziness as a chosen posture rather than an unavoidable trait. The beliyyaal, worthless person, of verse 12 names a moral category so severe it later became a title for embodied evil itself, Belial. And the closing list names things toebah, detestable, to the LORD, a covenantal term of the strongest kind, showing that pride, violence, deceit, and discord are treated with the same moral seriousness as idolatry elsewhere in Scripture.

typology.type: A false witness who gives false testimony, Proverbs 6:19
typology.fulfillment: The false witnesses sought against Jesus at His trial, Matthew 26:59-60, the innocent Son of God condemned by the very sin God is said to hate.

study.christ: The list of seven things the LORD hates reads almost like a preview of Christ's own passion, haughty men, a lying tongue, hands eager to shed innocent blood, false witnesses, and discord among brothers all converge at His trial and crucifixion, where religious leaders sought false testimony against Jesus, Matthew 26:59 to 60, and shed the blood of the only truly innocent man who ever lived. In bearing that very conspiracy of hated sins, Christ absorbed their judgment, so that both the guilty schemer and the wronged victim can find forgiveness and peace in Him.
study.god: God's moral character is not abstract in this passage, He has specific, named hatreds, pride, lying, violence, scheming, eagerness for evil, false testimony, and discord, each one a real threat to truth, life, or community. Yet the same God patiently instructs the reckless debtor and the sluggard toward a better way rather than abandoning them the moment they stumble.

crossReferences.note: False witnesses were sought against Jesus, the very sin named as detestable in verse 19.
If possible, live at peace with everyone, standing against the discord sowing named in verse 19.
God opposes the proud, echoing the haughty eyes God hates in verse 17.
What accord has Christ with Belial, using the same Hebrew term as the worthless person of verse 12 as a title for evil itself.

study.oneStory: This section's very practical warnings, on debt, laziness, scheming, and God's own list of hated sins, show wisdom reaching into every corner of ordinary life. That list of hatreds, above all false witness and shed innocent blood, was turned in full force against the innocent Christ, who bore it at the cross so that His people could be freed from both practicing and suffering these very sins.

quiz.q: What urgent action does verse 3 to 5 counsel for someone who has recklessly pledged security for another?

options.text: Go humbly and urgently to free yourself from the pledge without delay.
options.note: The passage's language, allow no sleep, free yourself like a gazelle, stresses urgency and humility.

options.text: Ignore it, since such pledges carry no real risk.
options.note: The passage treats this as a serious, urgent trap, not something to ignore.

options.text: Wait patiently for the debt to resolve itself over time.
options.note: The text calls for immediate, active effort, not passive waiting.

options.text: Take legal action against the neighbor immediately.
options.note: The passage instead counsels humbling oneself and pleading with the neighbor directly, not adversarial legal action.

quiz.q: What lesson does the ant teach the sluggard, verses 6 to 8?

options.text: Diligent work does not require external supervision to get done.
options.note: Verse 7 stresses the ant works without commander, overseer, or ruler.

options.text: Hard work is unnecessary if you are naturally gifted.
options.note: The passage's whole point is the necessity and value of diligent effort.

options.text: Rest is always wrong and should be entirely avoided.
options.note: The rebuke targets excessive, self ruining laziness, not rest as such.

options.text: Community leadership is the only path to productivity.
options.note: The passage's point is nearly the opposite, the ant is productive precisely without any leader directing it.

quiz.q: How does the passage describe the 'worthless person' in verses 12 to 15?

options.text: A schemer who sows discord through deceptive body language and faces sudden, irrecoverable ruin.
options.note: This matches the description exactly, winking, pointing, plotting evil, and sudden calamity.

options.text: Someone who is simply poor and unfortunate.
options.note: The passage's concern is moral character and scheming, not poverty.

options.text: A respected elder whose counsel is trusted.
options.note: The passage portrays the opposite, a deceitful troublemaker, not a trusted elder.

options.text: A careless but harmless gossip.
options.note: This understates the passage's description of active, deceitful scheming toward evil, not mere careless talk.

quiz.q: According to verses 16 to 19, which of the following is among the things the LORD hates?

options.text: A false witness who gives false testimony and one who stirs up discord among brothers.
options.note: Both appear explicitly in the closing list, verse 19.

options.text: Honest confession of sin.
options.note: Honest confession is commended throughout Scripture, the opposite of what this list condemns.

options.text: Careful and diligent labor.
options.note: Diligent labor is commended earlier in this very passage through the example of the ant, not condemned.

options.text: Humility before God and others.
options.note: Humility is the opposite of the haughty eyes this list actually condemns; it is commended, not hated, throughout Proverbs.

quiz.q: How does the list of things the LORD hates connect to the trial of Jesus?

options.text: False witnesses and hands eager to shed innocent blood, both named as detestable, converged against Jesus at His trial.
options.note: Matthew 26:59-60 records false witnesses sought against Jesus, matching the sins this list names.

options.text: It has no connection to the Gospel accounts at all.
options.note: The Gospel trial narratives directly display the very sins this list condemns.

options.text: It shows that Jesus Himself committed these sins.
options.note: The Gospels present Jesus as entirely innocent, the victim of these sins, not their perpetrator.

options.text: It only applies to Old Testament legal disputes, with no New Testament relevance.
options.note: This understates the direct connection the Gospel writers draw between false testimony against Jesus and exactly this kind of sin.

questions: The passage urges urgent, humble action to escape a reckless financial entanglement. Is there a commitment or debt you have been avoiding dealing with, hoping it will resolve on its own?
The ant works diligently without any supervisor watching. Where in your life are you tempted to coast, doing your best work only when someone is checking on you?

The worthless schemer plots evil through subtle body language, winking, pointing, before sudden ruin. Are there small, seemingly minor ways you manipulate or mislead others that could be quietly building toward real harm?
Of the seven things the LORD hates, which one do you most need to examine in your own heart this week, pride, lying, violence, scheming, eagerness for evil, false testimony, or stirring up discord?

Christ bore the very sins this passage names, false witness and shed innocent blood, at His own trial. How does knowing He absorbed these exact sins change how you view both your own guilt and those who have wronged you?

translationNotes.note: Press your plea can also be rendered hasten, or urge, stressing the urgency of resolving a reckless pledge without delay.
The pattern 'six things, seven' is a common device in Hebrew poetry for a complete or representative list, not a claim that God hates only these seven things and nothing more.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Proverbs 6:1-19. Poetic line breaks within verses 17 to 19 in the source were rendered as flowing prose; no em or en dashes were present in this passage and none are used anywhere in the file.
